title: Course schedule: return one valid order.
description: Course schedule: return one valid order.

examples.explanation:
Step 1: Start with empty path [].
Step 2: Try course 0: path=[0], used=[True,False,False,False]. Check prerequisites: none require 0 before others, so valid.
Step 3: Recursively backtrack from [0]. Try course 1: path=[0,1], used=[True,True,False,False]. Check [1,0] requires 0 before 1: satisfied. Check others: [2,0] and [3,1],[3,2] don't involve 1 yet, so valid.
Step 4: From [0,1], try course 2: path=[0,1,2], used=[True,True,True,False]. Check [2,0]: satisfied. Check [3,1]: requires 1 before 3 (not yet). Check [3,2]: requires 2 before 3 (not yet). So valid.
Step 5: From [0,1,2], try course 3: path=[0,1,2,3], used all True. Validate full sequence: [1,0] OK, [2,0] OK, [3,1] OK (1 at index1, 3 at index3), [3,2] OK (2 at index2, 3 at index3). Return [0,1,2,3].

Step 1: Start with empty path [].
Step 2: Try course 0: path=[0], used=[True,False]. Check prerequisites: [1,0] requires 0 before 1, not violated.
Step 3: From [0], try course 1: path=[0,1], used all True. Validate: [1,0] satisfied (0 before 1). Return [0,1].
Alternative path starting with 1 would fail early: path=[1], check [1,0] requires 0 before 1, but 0 not before, so backtrack immediately.

approaches.name: Brute Force Backtracking O(n! * n)
approaches.summary: Generate all permutations of courses and check each for validity by verifying prerequisites in order. Use backtracking to explore all possible sequences and return the first valid one.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Backtracking for Course Schedule
ladders.desc: Generate all permutations of courses and validate each against prerequisites using backtracking to find one valid order.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the course scheduling problem by exploring all possible orderings of courses and checking which ones satisfy all prerequisite constraints. The intuition is that if we try every possible sequence, we're guaranteed to find a valid one if it exists. We use backtracking to systematically generate permutations: start with an empty sequence, recursively add courses not yet included, and at each step check if the current partial sequence violates any prerequisites. When we reach a complete sequence of all courses, we verify it fully against all prerequisites. The algorithm backtracks when a course addition creates an invalid partial sequence or when all possibilities from that branch are exhausted. Time complexity is O(n! * n) because we generate n! permutations and each validation takes O(n) time to check all prerequisites. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and storing the current sequence. This connects to L1 by using the permutation generation as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include not pruning invalid partial sequences early (which dramatically increases runtime) and forgetting to mark courses as used/unused during backtracking. Real code pattern: def find_order(numCourses, prerequisites): result = []; used = [False]*numCourses; def backtrack(path): if len(path)==numCourses: if validate(path): result.append(path[:]); return; for i in range(numCourses): if not used[i]: if valid_addition(path, i): used[i]=True; path.append(i); backtrack(path); path.pop(); used[i]=False; backtrack([]); return result[0] if result else [].

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with empty path [].
Step 2: Try course 0: path=[0], used=[True,False,False,False]. Check prerequisites: none require 0 before others, so valid.
Step 3: Recursively backtrack from [0]. Try course 1: path=[0,1], used=[True,True,False,False]. Check [1,0] requires 0 before 1: satisfied. Check others: [2,0] and [3,1],[3,2] don't involve 1 yet, so valid.
Step 4: From [0,1], try course 2: path=[0,1,2], used=[True,True,True,False]. Check [2,0]: satisfied. Check [3,1]: requires 1 before 3 (not yet). Check [3,2]: requires 2 before 3 (not yet). So valid.
Step 5: From [0,1,2], try course 3: path=[0,1,2,3], used all True. Validate full sequence: [1,0] OK, [2,0] OK, [3,1] OK (1 at index1, 3 at index3), [3,2] OK (2 at index2, 3 at index3). Return [0,1,2,3].

Step 1: Start with empty path [].
Step 2: Try course 0: path=[0], used=[True,False]. Check prerequisites: [1,0] requires 0 before 1, not violated.
Step 3: From [0], try course 1: path=[0,1], used all True. Validate: [1,0] satisfied (0 before 1). Return [0,1].
Alternative path starting with 1 would fail early: path=[1], check [1,0] requires 0 before 1, but 0 not before, so backtrack immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generating All Permutations with Backtracking
ladders.desc: Generate all possible orderings of n distinct courses using recursive backtracking.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine creates every permutation of courses, which is essential for the brute force approach. The intuition is that by recursively building sequences and backtracking, we can systematically explore all n! possible orderings without repetition. The algorithm maintains a current path (partial permutation) and a used array to track which courses are already included. At each recursive call, if the path length equals n, we have a complete permutation. Otherwise, we iterate through all courses not yet used, add each to the path, mark it as used, recurse, then backtrack by removing it and marking unused. This depth-first exploration ensures all permutations are generated. Time complexity is O(n!) since there are n! permutations, and each base case takes O(1) to record. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth and the path/used arrays. This feeds into L0 by providing the permutations that will be validated against prerequisites. Common mistakes include not copying the path when storing permutations (leading to reference issues) and incorrect backtracking that doesn't properly unmark courses. Real code pattern: def generate_permutations(n): result = []; used = [False]*n; def backtrack(path): if len(path)==n: result.append(path[:]); return; for i in range(n): if not used[i]: used[i]=True; path.append(i); backtrack(path); path.pop(); used[i]=False; backtrack([]); return result.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start path=[], used=[F,F,F].
Step 2: Add 0: path=[0], used=[T,F,F]. Recurse.
Step 3: From [0], add 1: path=[0,1], used=[T,T,F]. Recurse.
Step 4: From [0,1], add 2: path=[0,1,2], used all T. Base case: store [0,1,2]. Backtrack: remove 2, used[2]=F.
Step 5: From [0,1], loop ends, backtrack: remove 1, used[1]=F.
Step 6: From [0], next i=2: path=[0,2], used=[T,F,T]. Recurse, add 1: path=[0,2,1], store. Backtrack similarly.
Step 7: After exploring all from [0], backtrack to [], then start with 1, etc., generating all 6 permutations.

Step 1: Start path=[], used=[F,F].
Step 2: Add 0: path=[0], used=[T,F]. Recurse.
Step 3: From [0], add 1: path=[0,1], used all T. Store [0,1]. Backtrack.
Step 4: From [0], loop ends, backtrack to [].
Step 5: Add 1: path=[1], used=[F,T]. Recurse, add 0: path=[1,0], store. Backtrack. Result has 2 permutations.

testCases.expected: 24 permutations total (list omitted for brevity, but verified count)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Validating a Course Sequence Against Prerequisites
ladders.desc: Check if a given sequence of courses satisfies all prerequisite constraints.
ladders.explanation: This is the core validation logic that determines whether a candidate course order is valid. The intuition is that for each prerequisite [a,b] (meaning course a requires b), we need to ensure b appears before a in the sequence. We can do this efficiently by creating a position map that stores the index of each course in the sequence. Then, for each prerequisite, we compare the indices: if position[b] < position[a], the prerequisite is satisfied; otherwise, it's violated. The algorithm takes the sequence and prerequisites as input, builds the position map in O(n) time, then iterates through prerequisites to check each in O(1) time, resulting in O(n + p) time where p is the number of prerequisites. Space complexity is O(n) for the map. This feeds into L0 by being the check applied to each generated permutation. Common mistakes include not handling duplicate prerequisites or assuming prerequisites are valid indices. Real code pattern: def is_valid_sequence(seq, prerequisites): pos = {course: idx for idx, course in enumerate(seq)}; for a, b in prerequisites: if pos.get(a, -1) <= pos.get(b, -1): return False; return True.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Build position map: {0:0, 1:1, 2:2, 3:3}.
Step 2: Check [1,0]: pos[1]=1, pos[0]=0, 0<1 so OK.
Step 3: Check [2,0]: 0<2 OK.
Step 4: Check [3,1]: 1<3 OK.
Step 5: Check [3,2]: 2<3 OK. All satisfied, return True.

Step 1: Build map: {1:0, 0:1, 2:2}.
Step 2: Check [0,1]: pos[0]=1, pos[1]=0, 0<1 is false (1 is not less than 0), so return False immediately.

ladders.title: Building Block — Early Pruning in Backtracking
ladders.desc: During permutation generation, check if adding a course maintains validity of prerequisites so far.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept optimizes the brute force approach by pruning invalid partial sequences early, rather than waiting to validate complete permutations. The intuition is that if a partial sequence already violates a prerequisite, any extension will also be invalid, so we can backtrack immediately. For example, if we have [1] and a prerequisite [1,0] requires 0 before 1, but 0 is not in the sequence yet, this is okay temporarily. But if we have [1,0] and [0,1] requires 1 before 0, that's violated. The algorithm modifies the permutation generator to, before adding a course to the path, check all prerequisites involving that course: for each prerequisite [a,b] where a is the course being added, if b is already in the path and appears after a, it's invalid; similarly, if b is the course being added and a is in the path before b, it's invalid. This check takes O(p) time per addition, but significantly reduces the search space. Time complexity becomes O(n! * p) in worst case, but with pruning it's often better. Space complexity remains O(n). This feeds into L0 by making the full algorithm more efficient. Common mistakes include incorrect logic in checking prerequisites or not considering both directions. Real code pattern: def can_add(course, path, prerequisites): for a, b in prerequisites: if a==course and b in path and path.index(b) > path.index(course): return False; if b==course and a in path and path.index(a) < path.index(course): return False; return True; used in backtracking: if can_add(i, path, prerequisites): ...

examples.trace: Step 1: Check prerequisites involving course 1: [1,0]. Since a=1 matches course, check if b=0 is in path: yes, at index 0. Condition: path.index(0)=0 > path.index(1) would be invalid, but 1 is not in path yet (we're adding it), so this check doesn't apply. Actually, we need to simulate adding: if we add 1, path becomes [0,1]. Check [1,0]: 0 is before 1, so valid. Return True.

Step 1: Check prerequisites involving course 0: [1,0] has b=0. Since b matches course, check if a=1 is in path: yes, at index 0. Condition: if we add 0, path becomes [1,0]. Check [1,0]: requires 0 before 1, but 1 is before 0, so invalid. Thus, can_add returns False, pruning this branch.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking a Single Prerequisite in a Sequence
ladders.desc: Determine if two courses satisfy a prerequisite relationship based on their positions in a sequence.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks whether a specific prerequisite [a,b] (meaning a requires b) is satisfied in a given sequence. The intuition is simple: b must appear before a in the sequence. Given a sequence and the indices of courses a and b, we compare them: if index(b) < index(a), the prerequisite is satisfied; otherwise, it's violated. The algorithm involves finding the positions of a and b in the sequence, which can be done with a linear scan or precomputed map. Time complexity is O(n) if scanning, O(1) with a map. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L2 as the basic check used repeatedly in validation. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors or not handling cases where a or b is not in the sequence. Real code pattern: def check_prereq(seq, a, b): try: idx_a = seq.index(a); idx_b = seq.index(b); return idx_b < idx_a; except ValueError: return False # or handle as needed.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Find index of a=3: seq.index(3)=3.
Step 2: Find index of b=1: seq.index(1)=1.
Step 3: Compare: 1 < 3 is true, so prerequisite [3,1] is satisfied. Return True.

Step 1: Index of a=0 is 1.
Step 2: Index of b=2 is 0.
Step 3: Compare: 0 < 1 is true, wait this means b before a, so prerequisite [0,2] requires 2 before 0, but 2 is at index0 and 0 at index1, so 0<1 is true, meaning 2 is before 0, so actually satisfied? Let's clarify: prerequisite [a,b] means a requires b, so need b before a. Here a=0, b=2, so need 2 before 0. Indexes: 2 at 0, 0 at 1, so 0<1 true, so satisfied. But output should be True. I made an error: example says output False, but trace shows True. Correcting: if prerequisite is [0,2], then need 2 before 0, which is true in [2,0,1], so output True. For False example, use [0,2] in sequence [0,2,1]: index of 0=0, index of 2=1, 1<0 false, so False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Prerequisite Relationships
ladders.desc: Learn how prerequisites define ordering constraints between courses.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding that a prerequisite [a,b] means course a requires course b to be taken first. In other words, b must come before a in the schedule. For example, if you have prerequisite [Calculus, Algebra], it means you need to take Algebra before Calculus. This creates a directed relationship: b -> a. The intuition is that these constraints form a partial order on courses. The basic operation is to read a list of such pairs and interpret them. There's no algorithm here per se, just comprehension. Time and space are O(1) for understanding a single pair. This feeds into L4 as the foundation for checking prerequisites. Common mistakes include confusing the order (thinking [a,b] means a before b) or not recognizing that multiple prerequisites can exist. Real code pattern: just a comment or data structure: prerequisites = [[a,b], ...] where each [a,b] means a requires b.

examples.output: Course 1 requires course 0 to be taken first.
examples.trace:
Step 1: Identify a=1, b=0.
Step 2: Interpret: a requires b, so course 1 needs course 0 before it.
Step 3: In a schedule, 0 must appear before 1.

examples.output: Course 2 requires course 1 first; course 3 requires course 2 first.
examples.trace:
Step 1: First pair [2,1]: course 2 requires course 1.
Step 2: Second pair [3,2]: course 3 requires course 2.
Step 3: Combined, this implies an order: 1 before 2 before 3.

testCases.expected: Course 1 requires course 0 first; course 2 requires course 0 first.
Course 4 requires course 2 first; course 2 requires course 1 first.
Course 7 requires course 7 first (self-loop, invalid in practice).
Course 0 requires course 1 first; course 1 requires course 2 first; course 2 requires course 3 first.
Course 3 requires course 0 first; course 0 requires course 3 first (contradiction).
Course 6 requires course 5 first; course 8 requires course 6 first; course 9 requires course 8 first.

approaches.name: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS) O(V + E)
approaches.summary: Model courses as nodes and prerequisites as directed edges in a graph. Use Kahn's algorithm with a queue to repeatedly remove nodes with zero indegree, building a topological order. Return the order if all nodes are processed.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Course Schedule with Kahn's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete topological sorting solution using Kahn's algorithm to find a valid course order.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete Course Schedule problem where we need to return one valid topological order of courses given prerequisites. The intuition is to model courses as nodes and prerequisites as directed edges (prerequisite → course) in a directed graph. Kahn's algorithm works by repeatedly removing nodes with zero indegree (courses with no remaining prerequisites), adding them to the topological order, and updating indegrees of their neighbors. This process naturally finds a valid order if one exists. The algorithm begins by building an adjacency list and calculating indegrees for all nodes. We use a queue to store nodes with indegree 0. While the queue isn't empty, we dequeue a node, add it to the result, and for each neighbor, decrement its indegree. If a neighbor's indegree becomes 0, we enqueue it. If the result contains all nodes, we return it; otherwise, we return an empty list indicating a cycle. Time complexity is O(V + E) where V is number of courses and E is prerequisites. Space complexity is O(V + E) for adjacency list and indegree array. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete topological sort routine. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize indegree array properly, not handling cycles correctly, or using wrong graph direction. Real code pattern: def findOrder(numCourses, prerequisites): graph = [[] for _ in range(numCourses)]; indegree = [0]*numCourses; for course, prereq in prerequisites: graph[prereq].append(course); indegree[course] += 1; queue = deque([i for i in range(numCourses) if indegree[i]==0]); result = []; while queue: node = queue.popleft(); result.append(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: indegree[neighbor] -= 1; if indegree[neighbor]==0: queue.append(neighbor); return result if len(result)==numCourses else [].

examples.trace: Step 1: Build graph: 0→[1,2], 1→[3], 2→[3], 3→[]. Indegree: [0,1,1,2]. Step 2: Initialize queue with nodes indegree 0: [0]. Result=[]. Step 3: Dequeue 0, add to result: [0]. Update neighbors: indegree[1]=0, enqueue 1; indegree[2]=0, enqueue 2. Queue=[1,2]. Step 4: Dequeue 1, add to result: [0,1]. Update neighbor 3: indegree[3]=1. Queue=[2]. Step 5: Dequeue 2, add to result: [0,1,2]. Update neighbor 3: indegree[3]=0, enqueue 3. Queue=[3]. Step 6: Dequeue 3, add to result: [0,1,2,3]. Queue empty. Result has 4 nodes, return [0,1,2,3].

Step 1: Build graph: 0→[1], 1→[2], 2→[0]. Indegree: [1,1,1]. Step 2: No nodes with indegree 0, queue empty initially. Result=[]. Step 3: While loop never executes. Result length 0 ≠ 3, return [] indicating cycle.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Topological Sort with Kahn's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement Kahn's algorithm for topological sorting on a given directed graph.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements Kahn's algorithm to perform topological sorting on a directed graph. The intuition is that in a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG), there must be at least one node with no incoming edges (indegree 0). By repeatedly removing such nodes and their outgoing edges, we can construct a linear ordering. The algorithm takes an adjacency list and number of nodes as input. First, calculate indegree for each node by traversing the adjacency list. Initialize a queue with all nodes having indegree 0. While the queue isn't empty, dequeue a node, add it to the result, and for each neighbor, decrement its indegree. If a neighbor's indegree becomes 0, enqueue it. If the result contains all nodes, return it; otherwise, return empty list indicating a cycle. Time complexity is O(V + E). Space complexity is O(V) for indegree array and queue. This feeds into L0 by providing the core topological sort logic for the course schedule problem. Common mistakes include not updating indegrees correctly or missing cycle detection. Real code pattern: def topologicalSort(graph, n): indegree = [0]*n; for u in range(n): for v in graph[u]: indegree[v] += 1; queue = deque([i for i in range(n) if indegree[i]==0]); result = []; while queue: u = queue.popleft(); result.append(u); for v in graph[u]: indegree[v] -= 1; if indegree[v]==0: queue.append(v); return result if len(result)==n else [].

examples.trace: Step 1: Calculate indegree: node 0:0, 1:1, 2:1, 3:2. Step 2: Queue with indegree 0: [0]. Result=[]. Step 3: Dequeue 0, result=[0]. Update neighbors 1,2: indegree[1]=0, enqueue 1; indegree[2]=0, enqueue 2. Queue=[1,2]. Step 4: Dequeue 1, result=[0,1]. Update neighbor 3: indegree[3]=1. Queue=[2]. Step 5: Dequeue 2, result=[0,1,2]. Update neighbor 3: indegree[3]=0, enqueue 3. Queue=[3]. Step 6: Dequeue 3, result=[0,1,2,3]. Queue empty. Result length 4, return [0,1,2,3].
Step 1: Calculate indegree: node 0:1, 1:1, 2:1. Step 2: No nodes with indegree 0, queue empty. Result=[]. Step 3: While loop never executes. Result length 0 ≠ 3, return [].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Processing Nodes with Zero Indegree
ladders.desc: Implement the BFS-like process of repeatedly removing nodes with indegree 0 and updating their neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the central mechanism of Kahn's algorithm: processing nodes with zero indegree in a BFS manner. The intuition is that nodes with no incoming dependencies can be scheduled immediately. By removing them and reducing indegrees of their neighbors, we make new nodes eligible for processing. The algorithm maintains a queue of nodes with indegree 0. While the queue isn't empty, we dequeue a node, add it to the order, and for each neighbor, decrement its indegree. If a neighbor's indegree becomes 0, we enqueue it. This process continues until the queue is empty. The key insight is that this BFS traversal naturally produces a topological order if the graph is acyclic. Time complexity for this sub-problem is O(V + E) as we process each node and edge once. Space complexity is O(V) for the queue. This feeds into L1 as the main loop of topological sort. Common mistakes include not using a queue (using a list with O(n) removals) or forgetting to check if indegree becomes exactly 0 before enqueueing. Real code pattern: def processZeroIndegree(graph, indegree): n = len(graph); queue = deque([i for i in range(n) if indegree[i]==0]); order = []; while queue: node = queue.popleft(); order.append(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: indegree[neighbor] -= 1; if indegree[neighbor] == 0: queue.append(neighbor); return order.

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue initialized with nodes indegree 0: [0]. Order=[]. Step 2: Dequeue 0, order=[0]. Update neighbors 1,2: indegree becomes [0,0,0,2]. Enqueue 1 and 2. Queue=[1,2]. Step 3: Dequeue 1, order=[0,1]. Update neighbor 3: indegree becomes [0,0,0,1]. Queue=[2]. Step 4: Dequeue 2, order=[0,1,2]. Update neighbor 3: indegree becomes [0,0,0,0]. Enqueue 3. Queue=[3]. Step 5: Dequeue 3, order=[0,1,2,3]. Queue empty. Return [0,1,2,3].
Step 1: No nodes with indegree 0, queue empty. Order=[]. Step 2: While loop never executes. Return [].

ladders.title: Building Block — Calculating Indegrees from Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Compute the indegree (number of incoming edges) for each node in a directed graph.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on calculating indegrees, which is essential for Kahn's algorithm. The intuition is that indegree represents how many prerequisites a node has. In a directed graph represented as an adjacency list, indegree of a node v is the number of times v appears as a destination in the adjacency lists of all nodes. The algorithm initializes an indegree array of size n with zeros. Then, for each node u, we iterate through its adjacency list (neighbors v) and increment indegree[v] by 1. This counts each edge exactly once. Time complexity is O(V + E) as we visit each node and each edge. Space complexity is O(V) for the indegree array. This feeds into L2 by providing the indegree array needed to identify nodes with zero indegree. Common mistakes include initializing indegree incorrectly (e.g., not setting to zero) or misunderstanding adjacency list direction. Real code pattern: def calculateIndegree(graph, n): indegree = [0]*n; for u in range(n): for v in graph[u]: indegree[v] += 1; return indegree.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize indegree=[0,0,0,0]. Step 2: For u=0, neighbors [1,2]: indegree[1]=1, indegree[2]=1. Step 3: For u=1, neighbors [3]: indegree[3]=2. Step 4: For u=2, neighbors [3]: indegree[3]=3? Wait, careful: indegree[3] was 2, now becomes 3? No, re-trace: Actually, after u=1: indegree=[0,1,1,2]. After u=2: indegree[3] becomes 3? Let's correct: graph[2]=[3], so indegree[3] increments from 2 to 3. But that's wrong based on graph. Let's trace properly: Start indegree=[0,0,0,0]. u=0: neighbors 1,2 → indegree[1]=1, indegree[2]=1. u=1: neighbor 3 → indegree[3]=1. u=2: neighbor 3 → indegree[3]=2. u=3: no neighbors. Final indegree=[0,1,1,2]. Yes, that's correct.
Step 1: Initialize indegree=[0,0,0]. Step 2: u=0, neighbor 1: indegree[1]=1. Step 3: u=1, neighbor 2: indegree[2]=1. Step 4: u=2, neighbor 0: indegree[0]=1. Final indegree=[1,1,1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Building Adjacency List from Edge List
ladders.desc: Convert a list of directed edges into an adjacency list representation of a graph.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves constructing an adjacency list, a fundamental graph representation. The intuition is that an adjacency list efficiently stores neighbors for each node, which is crucial for graph traversal algorithms. Given n nodes and a list of edges (each as [u,v] meaning directed edge from u to v), we create a list of n empty lists. Then, for each edge [u,v], we append v to the list at index u. This builds a mapping from each node to its outgoing neighbors. Time complexity is O(E) where E is number of edges. Space complexity is O(V + E) for the adjacency list. This feeds into L3 by providing the graph structure needed to calculate indegrees. Common mistakes include using wrong index (e.g., 1-based vs 0-based), not initializing empty lists, or misunderstanding edge direction. Real code pattern: def buildAdjacencyList(n, edges): graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u, v in edges: graph[u].append(v); return graph.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph=[[],[],[],[]]. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: append 1 to graph[0] → graph=[[1],[],[],[]]. Step 3: Edge [0,2]: append 2 to graph[0] → graph=[[1,2],[],[],[]]. Step 4: Edge [1,3]: append 3 to graph[1] → graph=[[1,2],[3],[],[]]. Step 5: Edge [2,3]: append 3 to graph[2] → graph=[[1,2],[3],[3],[]]. Final graph=[[1,2],[3],[3],[]].
Step 1: Initialize graph=[[],[],[]]. Step 2: Edge [0,1]: graph[0]=[1]. Step 3: Edge [1,2]: graph[1]=[2]. Step 4: Edge [2,0]: graph[2]=[0]. Final graph=[[1],[2],[0]].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs and Adjacency Lists
ladders.desc: Understand directed graphs and represent them using adjacency lists.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces directed graphs and their adjacency list representation, essential for graph algorithms. A directed graph consists of nodes (vertices) and directed edges (arrows) connecting them. Each edge has a direction: from a source node to a destination node. In the course schedule problem, nodes are courses, and a directed edge from course A to course B means A is a prerequisite for B. An adjacency list is a way to represent a graph where each node has a list of its outgoing neighbors. For example, if node 0 has edges to nodes 1 and 2, its adjacency list entry is [1,2]. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs and allows quick access to neighbors. To build it, we create a list of empty lists, one per node. Then, for each directed edge (u,v), we add v to the list at index u. Time complexity to build is O(E). Space complexity is O(V + E). This feeds into L4 as the basic structure for graph operations. Common mistakes include confusing directed with undirected graphs, or misinterpreting edge direction. Real code pattern: graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u, v in edges: graph[u].append(v).

examples.trace: Step 1: Create empty graph with 3 lists: [[],[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge [0,1]: append 1 to graph[0] → [[1],[],[]]. Step 3: Add edge [1,2]: append 2 to graph[1] → [[1],[2],[]]. Final graph represents: node 0 points to 1, node 1 points to 2, node 2 points to none.
Step 1: Create empty graph: [[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge [1,0]: append 0 to graph[1] → [[],[0]]. Final graph: node 0 points to none, node 1 points to 0.

approaches.name: DFS Topological Sort O(V + E)
approaches.summary: Perform a depth-first search on the graph, marking nodes as visiting or visited to detect cycles. Build the order by adding nodes to a stack after processing all dependencies, ensuring prerequisites come first.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Course Schedule Order with DFS Topological Sort
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find a valid course order using DFS topological sort with cycle detection.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete problem where we need to find a valid ordering of courses given prerequisites. The intuition is that courses form a directed graph where edges represent prerequisites. A valid order exists if and only if there's no cycle in the graph (no circular dependencies). We use DFS topological sort because it naturally handles cycle detection while building the order. The algorithm works by performing DFS on each unvisited node, marking nodes as 'visiting' during recursion and 'visited' after processing. If we encounter a node marked as 'visiting' during DFS, we've found a cycle. After processing all dependencies of a node, we add it to a stack. The stack will contain nodes in reverse topological order (prerequisites at the bottom, dependent courses at the top). When popped, we get the correct order. Step-by-step: 1) Build adjacency list from prerequisites. 2) Initialize visited array with three states: unvisited (0), visiting (1), visited (2). 3) For each node, if unvisited, run DFS. 4) In DFS: mark as visiting, recursively process neighbors, if any neighbor is visiting → cycle detected, after processing neighbors mark as visited and push to stack. 5) If no cycle, pop stack to get order. Time: O(V+E) for building graph and DFS traversal. Space: O(V+E) for adjacency list, visited array, and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This uses the DFS helper function to process each node. Common mistakes: Not handling cycles properly, forgetting to mark nodes as visited after processing, incorrect graph construction. Real code pattern: def findOrder(numCourses, prerequisites): graph = [[] for _ in range(numCourses)]; for course, prereq in prerequisites: graph[prereq].append(course); visited = [0]*numCourses; result = []; def dfs(node): if visited[node] == 1: return False; if visited[node] == 2: return True; visited[node] = 1; for neighbor in graph[node]: if not dfs(neighbor): return False; visited[node] = 2; result.append(node); return True; for i in range(numCourses): if visited[i] == 0: if not dfs(i): return []; return result[::-1]

examples.trace: Step 1: Build graph: 0→[1,2], 1→[3], 2→[3], 3→[]. Step 2: visited=[0,0,0,0]. Step 3: Start DFS at 0: mark 0 as visiting (1). Process neighbor 1: mark 1 as visiting. Process neighbor 3: mark 3 as visiting. No neighbors, mark 3 as visited (2), push 3 to stack. Back to 1: mark 1 as visited, push 1. Back to 0: process neighbor 2: mark 2 as visiting. Process neighbor 3: already visited, skip. Mark 2 as visited, push 2. Back to 0: mark 0 as visited, push 0. Stack: [3,1,2,0]. Step 4: Pop stack: order = [0,2,1,3]. Alternative order [0,1,2,3] possible depending on neighbor processing order.

Step 1: Build graph: 0→[1], 1→[0]. Step 2: visited=[0,0]. Step 3: Start DFS at 0: mark 0 as visiting. Process neighbor 1: mark 1 as visiting. Process neighbor 0: 0 is visiting → cycle detected. Return False. Step 4: Return empty list.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS with Three-State Cycle Detection
ladders.desc: DFS function that marks nodes as unvisited, visiting, or visited to detect cycles during traversal.
ladders.explanation: This is the core DFS helper function used in the full solution. The intuition is that during DFS, we need to distinguish between nodes currently being explored (in the recursion stack) and nodes fully processed. We use three states: 0=unvisited, 1=visiting (currently in recursion stack), 2=visited (fully processed). When we start DFS on a node, we mark it as visiting. Then we recursively process all its neighbors. If we encounter a neighbor marked as visiting, we've found a back edge, indicating a cycle. After processing all neighbors, we mark the node as visited. This approach efficiently detects cycles in directed graphs. Step-by-step: 1) Check if node is visiting → return false (cycle). 2) Check if node is visited → return true (already processed). 3) Mark node as visiting. 4) For each neighbor, recursively call DFS. If any returns false, propagate false. 5) Mark node as visited, add to result stack, return true. Time: O(V+E) in total across all calls. Space: O(V) for visited array and recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is called for each node in the main algorithm. Connection to L2: This implements the three-state marking technique. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check for visited nodes early (causing repeated work), not propagating cycle detection correctly. Real code pattern: def dfs(node): if visited[node] == 1: return False; if visited[node] == 2: return True; visited[node] = 1; for neighbor in graph[node]: if not dfs(neighbor): return False; visited[node] = 2; result.append(node); return True

examples.input: graph = [[1,2],[3],[3],[]], start node = 0, visited initially all 0
examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(0): visited[0]=0 → mark as visiting (1). Step 2: Process neighbor 1: dfs(1): visited[1]=0 → mark as visiting. Process neighbor 3: dfs(3): visited[3]=0 → mark as visiting. No neighbors → mark 3 as visited (2), push 3. Return to 1: mark 1 as visited, push 1. Return to 0: process neighbor 2: dfs(2): visited[2]=0 → mark as visiting. Process neighbor 3: visited[3]=2 → return true. Mark 2 as visited, push 2. Return to 0: mark 0 as visited, push 0. Return true.

examples.input: graph = [[1],[0]], start node = 0, visited initially all 0
examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(0): visited[0]=0 → mark as visiting. Step 2: Process neighbor 1: dfs(1): visited[1]=0 → mark as visiting. Process neighbor 0: visited[0]=1 → return false. Propagate false back to dfs(0). Return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Three-State Marking for Cycle Detection in Directed Graphs
ladders.desc: Using three states (unvisited, visiting, visited) to detect cycles during graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique for cycle detection in directed graphs during DFS. The intuition is that in a directed graph, a cycle exists if during DFS we encounter a node that's currently in the recursion stack (i.e., we're still processing its descendants). We track this with three states: 0=unvisited (not yet started), 1=visiting (currently in recursion stack), 2=visited (fully processed). When we start processing a node, we mark it as visiting. As we recursively process its neighbors, if we find a neighbor marked as visiting, we've found a back edge → cycle. After processing all neighbors, we mark it as visited. This works because in a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph), all edges go from visited nodes to unvisited nodes or within the same DFS tree, but never to a visiting node. The three states ensure we don't reprocess visited nodes (efficiency) and correctly detect cycles. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize all nodes as unvisited (0). 2) When entering a node: if visiting → cycle; if visited → skip; else mark as visiting. 3) Process neighbors recursively. 4) After neighbors: mark as visited. Time: O(1) per state check. Space: O(V) for state array. Connection to L1: This is the mechanism used in the DFS function. Connection to L3: This builds on the concept of graph traversal. Common mistakes: Using only two states (visited/unvisited) which can't detect cycles correctly, not resetting states properly. Real code pattern: visited = [0]*n; def dfs(u): if visited[u] == 1: return False; if visited[u] == 2: return True; visited[u] = 1; for v in graph[u]: if not dfs(v): return False; visited[u] = 2; return True

examples.trace: Step 1: Start dfs(0): visited[0]=0 → set to 1. Step 2: dfs(1): visited[1]=0 → set to 1. Step 3: dfs(2): visited[2]=0 → set to 1. Step 4: dfs(3): visited[3]=0 → set to 1. No neighbors → set visited[3]=2. Step 5: Back to dfs(2): set visited[2]=2. Step 6: Back to dfs(1): set visited[1]=2. Step 7: Back to dfs(0): set visited[0]=2. All nodes visited, no visiting nodes encountered.

examples.output: Cycle detected when trying to visit 0 from 1
examples.trace: Step 1: Start dfs(0): visited[0]=0 → set to 1. Step 2: dfs(1): visited[1]=0 → set to 1. Step 3: From 1, try dfs(0): visited[0]=1 → cycle detected. Return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Depth-First Search (DFS) on Directed Graphs
ladders.desc: Traversing a directed graph using depth-first search to explore all reachable nodes.
ladders.explanation: DFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. For directed graphs, we follow edges in their direction. The intuition is to start at a node, recursively visit all its neighbors, marking nodes as visited to avoid reprocessing. This ensures we explore all nodes reachable from the starting point. In the context of topological sort, DFS helps process dependencies before dependents. Step-by-step: 1) Choose an unvisited node. 2) Mark it as visited. 3) For each outgoing edge to a neighbor, if the neighbor is unvisited, recursively apply DFS. 4) After processing all neighbors, the node is fully processed. This creates a depth-first order. Time: O(V+E) where V is nodes, E is edges. Space: O(V) for visited set and recursion stack. Connection to L2: DFS provides the traversal framework where we add three-state marking. Connection to L4: DFS uses adjacency lists to access neighbors. Common mistakes: Not handling disconnected graphs, infinite recursion in cyclic graphs without visited checks. Real code pattern: visited = set(); def dfs(node): visited.add(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited: dfs(neighbor)

examples.output: Visited nodes in DFS order: 0,1,2,3 or 0,3,1,2
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 0, mark visited. Step 2: Process neighbor 1 (first edge): visit 1, mark visited. Step 3: From 1, process neighbor 2: visit 2, mark visited. Step 4: Back to 0, process next neighbor 3: visit 3, mark visited. Order depends on neighbor processing order.

Step 1: Start at 0, mark visited. Step 2: Process neighbor 1: visit 1, mark visited. Step 3: From 1, process neighbor 2: visit 2, mark visited. Step 4: From 2, try neighbor 0 but 0 already visited, skip. Done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Building and Traversing an Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Creating an adjacency list representation of a directed graph and accessing neighbors.
ladders.explanation: An adjacency list is a way to represent a graph where each node has a list of its outgoing neighbors. For directed graphs, edge (u,v) means u→v. The intuition is that it's space-efficient for sparse graphs and allows quick access to neighbors. Building it: Given a list of edges (prerequisites), for each edge [v,u] (meaning u is prerequisite for v), we add v to the list of u's neighbors (since we want to traverse from prerequisite to dependent). Traversing: To access neighbors of a node, simply iterate through its list. This is fundamental for graph algorithms like DFS. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize empty list for each node. 2) For each edge [v,u]: append v to graph[u]. 3) To traverse: for neighbor in graph[node]: process neighbor. Time: O(E) to build, O(degree(node)) to access neighbors. Space: O(V+E). Connection to L3: DFS uses adjacency lists to find neighbors. Connection to L5: This builds on array/list operations. Common mistakes: Incorrect edge direction (prerequisites vs dependents), off-by-one errors with node indices. Real code pattern: n = numCourses; graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for course, prereq in prerequisites: graph[prereq].append(course)

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph = [[],[],[],[]]. Step 2: Process [1,0]: add 1 to graph[0] → [[1],[],[],[]]. Step 3: Process [2,0]: add 2 to graph[0] → [[1,2],[],[],[]]. Step 4: Process [3,1]: add 3 to graph[1] → [[1,2],[3],[],[]]. Step 5: Process [3,2]: add 3 to graph[2] → [[1,2],[3],[3],[]].
Step 1: Initialize graph = [[],[]]. Step 2: Process [1,0]: add 1 to graph[0] → [[1],[]].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays, Lists, and Basic Iteration
ladders.desc: Creating arrays/lists, accessing elements, and iterating with loops.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational programming concept needed for graph algorithms. Arrays (or lists in Python) are ordered collections of elements accessible by index. The intuition is that we can represent graph nodes as indices in an array, and store their neighbors in lists. Key operations: 1) Creating a list of size n: [0]*n or [[] for _ in range(n)]. 2) Accessing element at index i: list[i]. 3) Modifying element: list[i] = value. 4) Appending to list: list.append(value). 5) Iterating with for loops: for i in range(n): or for item in list:. These are building blocks for constructing adjacency lists and traversing graphs. Step-by-step: To create an adjacency list for n nodes: graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]. To add an edge from u to v: graph[u].append(v). To traverse all nodes: for i in range(n): process graph[i]. Time: O(1) for access/append, O(n) for iteration. Space: O(n) for the list. Connection to L4: This is used to build and use adjacency lists. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds, confusing 0-based vs 1-based indexing, modifying list while iterating. Real code pattern: n = 5; arr = [0]*n; arr[0] = 1; for i in range(n): print(arr[i]); lst = []; lst.append(10)

examples.input: Create list of 3 empty lists, add 5 to first list, iterate and print lengths
examples.trace: Step 1: graph = [[] for _ in range(3)] → [[],[],[]]. Step 2: graph[0].append(5) → [[5],[],[]]. Step 3: for i in range(3): print(len(graph[i])): i=0 → len([5])=1, i=1 → len([])=0, i=2 → len([])=0.
Step 1: arr = [10,20,30]. Step 2: for i in range(len(arr)): arr[i] *= 2. i=0 → arr[0]=20, i=1 → arr[1]=40, i=2 → arr[2]=60. Result: [20,40,60].

testCases.input: Create list of 4 zeros, set index 2 to 7
Create list of 3 empty lists using comprehension
Create list with elements 0 to 4 using range
List = [8,6,4], iterate and subtract 2 from each
